what_it_does: A React component that renders an interactive, multi-mode radial projection of corpus points onto a canvas. It fetches projection data (including live SSE updates), supports time, semantic, separator, nucleation, connection, and scale-ladder lenses, animates transitions, and lets the user pick points, build a working set, and hand it off.
observations: Uses a single canvas for all rendering, with device-pixel-ratio scaling and CSS custom-property palette read at draw time.

Implements several distinct geometric lenses in one draw routine: temporal rings, semantic meaning-distance, separator left/right basins, nucleation registry square, directed sector chords, and scale-ladder theme centroids.
Subscribes to /api/stream via EventSource for live reprojection and debounces bursts.
Recomputes the nucleation birth threshold client-side so the 20/80 dial feels instant without refetching.
Crossfades outgoing and incoming point sets when the scale rung changes.
Animates point positions on re-projection by tweening from previously drawn positions stored in refs.
Uses HSL angle-hue mapping as the primary point color, except in separator mode where color encodes pole.
Includes collision-avoided theme labels and edge-aware candidate-zone labels for narrow viewports.
Maintains a working set (sel) and a handoff callback for passing selections to a builder.
Draws a dyadic structural grid overlay and strain lines showing structure-meaning divergence.
standouts: The universal-projection conceit: one circular canvas substrate hosts radically different analytical geometries.
Live SSE-driven reprojection with identity-preserving animation and rung crossfade.
Separator mode uses spatial basins (left/right) and pole hues rather than color alone to show two-gravity separation.
Nucleation mode literally renders registered types inside a square and candidate new types piling outside it.
conventions: React functional component with hooks (useState, useEffect, useRef, useCallback).
Canvas rendering centralized in a single large useCallback draw() function.
Geometry helpers defined as closures inside the component so they share state and refs.
TypeScript interfaces describe the projection payload and point shape.
CSS design tokens read from :root via getComputedStyle inside draw().
Comments double as design rationale, naming groups (G9, G10, G11, S4) and referencing Tim Geldard/SEED.
concerns: One file mixes about six projection modes, making the draw() function long and mode-branch heavy.
Canvas hit testing likely duplicates geometry formulas from draw(), creating drift risk between rendering and picking.
Many magic numbers (radii, alphas, thresholds, label offsets) are scattered and undocumented.
Reading getComputedStyle on every draw frame is a small but unnecessary cost.
Two tick() overloads/symbols suggest the animation loop may be split or duplicated.
Client-side born() recomputation depends on server-provided size/distinct/pile_clustered; if server semantics change the UI verdict can diverge.
notes: A canvas-heavy, live-updating radial map that tries to make time, meaning, polarity, type-birth, typed edges, and thematic scale all readable in the same circular view.
questions: What is the exact contract of /api/projection and /api/stream beyond the query parameters shown?
How does pick() map mouse coordinates to points for each lens (its body is not shown)?
Where are bindings, default poles, and available spaces configured?
What does onHandoff do in the builder and what shape does sel take when handed off?
How are scale_children and source used in rendering?
What UI controls exist outside the canvas (the file is truncated after draw())?
purpose_vs_actual: The header claims a universal projection rendered from Tim Geldard's equation; the file does render multiple unified projection geometries, so the purpose largely matches, though 'universal' is an aspiration rather than a verifiable property.
apparent_themes: Geometry encodes meaning (angle = kind, radius = time/meaning/lean, color = angle or pole).
Circle-square duality (angular sectors versus dyadic structural grid).
Live data as a moving organ, not a static photograph.
Honest representation of unknowns (r_unknown points sit at the rim, never dropped).
Decluttering and legibility over completeness (label thinning, collision dropping).
Spatial separation as a primary signal, color as reinforcement.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to create a single visual substrate where every analytical lens over the same corpus can be explored without switching pages or charts, using polar coordinates and direct canvas control to make structural and temporal relations immediately visible.
novelty: The unusual aspect is a unified canvas component that switches between time-wheel, semantic field, two-pole gravity basins, nucleation square, chord-diagram connections, and scale-ladder theme centroids while preserving point identity through animation and crossfade; plus client-side recomputation of a birth threshold for instant dial feedback.
contribution: It contributes a self-contained, mode-rich canvas renderer and interaction controller that realizes the 'universal projection' front end, with live streaming, selection, animation, and lens-specific hit testing; a fused system would keep it as the concrete rendering layer for this multi-lens idea.
summary_for_embedding: LatticeView.tsx is a React canvas component that fetches a multi-mode radial corpus projection via SSE and renders time, semantic, separator, nucleation, connection, and scale-ladder lenses with animation, picking, and selection handoff.
intention_for_embedding: It was meant to provide one live, interactive circular view where every analytical lens—time, meaning, polarity, type-birth, typed edges, and thematic scale—is drawn in the same geometric substrate.

symbols.does: Describes a single projected corpus point.
symbols.description: Carries angular, radial, depth, structural cell, address, summary, timestamp, phase cycles, optional source, separator pulls/lean/pole, structural radius/strain, scale aggregation metadata, and nucleation fit flags. It is the universal atom rendered by the canvas.

symbols.does: Describes the entire projection payload returned by the bridge.
symbols.description: Includes now anchor, grid/rings, active binding, available bindings, sectors, points, optional separation report, directed edges, scale ladder, and nucleation report. It is the state that drives draw().
